DISCLAIMER
1
In this program you will find references and statements attributed to several hundred public figures and historical personages.

All such statements attributed to living persons are fictional; they are intended as gentle satire and provocative humor. In no case is there any implication that the statements reflect the true sentiments of the "alleged" speaker.

The 2000 situations portrayed in the SIMULATIONS MODE are totally fictitious and do not refer to actual events.

JOYSTICK
1
Do you wish to use a joystick while playing Mind Mirror? (Y)es or (N)o

The buttons on your joystick function as RETURN keys.

When letter responses are requested, moving the joystick left and right will provide them in the following manner:

^ JOYSTICK LEFT JOYSTICK RIGHT
^ (A)gree (D)isagree
^ (Y)es (N)o

For the Report Card in the Realm of Mental Abilities, joystick left and right step through a table of grades.

<CTRL0F>
PSYCHOLOGICAL CONSULTANT
1
MIND MIRROR can be a valuable tool in psychological work.
1. PSYCHO-DIAGNOSIS: Staff members can compare their views of a client.
2. INTERACTION: The client and you can compare thoughts.
3. CLIENT INSIGHT: Teach the client to Micro-Scope his/her views of self, history, significant others.
4. PROGRESS: Staff and client plot changes in course of counseling.
5. GROUP ENCOUNTERS: Group members compare perceptions.
6. SIMULATIONS: Put yourself and your client in a variety of roles. This can lead to valuable insights.

<CTRL0F>
PERSONNEL MANAGEMENT CONSULTANT
1
MIND MIRROR increases understanding of interpersonal relations. Personnel Management Consultants use it to assess:

1. JOB CHARACTERISTICS: Mind-Scope requirements for each job in a company.
2. STAFF APPRAISAL: Use Mind Mirror to compare employees/applicants with jobs.
3. STAFF INSIGHT: Employees and applicants compare selves with jobs.
4. COMPANY DYNAMICS: All employees scope views of relevant others.
5. DECISION MAKING: Plot your game moves.
6. MARKETING: Scope target-groups and compare with products.

<CTRL0F>
EDUCATIONAL CONSULTANT
1
The Electronic MIND MIRROR is a Thought-Making-Tool. Education usually consists of passing heavy "Thought-Stones" from generation to generation. These Macro-thoughts can be used to crush new ideas.

Now students can clarify and map their own thoughts about history, philosophy, science, politics, culture, themselves. Students' ideas can be plotted and compared with those of the teacher or other students. Learning becomes active, creative, inventive. The aim of the teacher in the Information Society is to help students THINK FOR THEMSELVES with precision and clarity.

choices.text_preview: EXERCISE 2. SELF-RANGE Compare your best and worst selves. # Return to previous menu.

prose:
SELF REFLECTIONS
"In a magic mirror we see reflected not only our lives, but the lives of all men that have been. When I think on this majestic theme, my eyes dazzle."
<,17>- Oliver Wendell Holmes

MIND MIRROR enables you to magnify and reflect your thoughts from day to day about any topic. Isn't precise thinking about your self the most basic tool for managing your life successfully?

Choose one of the following exercises in the art of accurate self-reflection.

#0EXERCISE 1. SELF PORTRAIT
Compare your self with your ideal.
#1EXERCISE 2. SELF-RANGE
Compare your best and worst selves.
# Return to previous menu.

EXERCISE 1. SELF PORTRAIT
1
In this exercise you'll be Mind-Scoping and comparing two subjects:

1. YOUR SELF: Scope your self as you are right now. Every time you boot up MIND MIRROR you may want to up-date your rapidly changing self-image!

2. YOUR IDEAL SELF: Here you can scope the person you want to become. Hopefully this ideal self will be changing too!

Your Self
Your Ideal Self
EXERCISE 2. SELF-RANGE
1
"It is no use to blame the mirror..."
<,25>- Nikolai Gogol

In this exercise you'll be Mind-Scoping and comparing two aspects of your intimate self.

1. YOUR BEST SELF: Bragging time. Mind-Scope your view of your self at the most successful moment of your life.

2. YOUR WORST SELF: Honesty time. Mind-Scope your self at the least successful moment of your life.

Your Best Self
Your Worst Self
Time to follow Shakespeare's advice, "To hold as 'twere the mirror up to nature, to show the mind her own feature." Micro-Scope your self in different situations and times in your life.

Self Stressed Self Drunk
Self Relaxed Self At Church
Self With Women Self Horny
Self With Men Self With Kids
Self At Work Self
Self At Play Ideal Self


choices.text_preview: EXERCISE 2. YOUR FATHER Compare him with your ideal Dad. # Return to previous menu.

prose:
AUTOBIOGRAPHY
"Le sort fait les parents, le choix fait les amis."
(Fate chooses our parents, we choose our friends.)
<,23>- Jacques Delille

In reconstructing your Life History, relationships with your parents are of crucial importance. Start by scoping the current situation. If your parents are not alive, scope them as you view them now.

Choose one of the following exercises.

#0EXERCISE 1. YOUR MOTHER
Compare her with your ideal Mom.
#1EXERCISE 2. YOUR FATHER
Compare him with your ideal Dad.
# Return to previous menu.

EXERCISE 1. YOUR MOTHER
1
Who ran to help you when you fell? And would some pretty story tell? Or kiss the place to make it well? Let's find out!

In this exercise you'll be Mind-Scoping and comparing two pregnant subjects.

1. YOUR MOTHER: Scope your mother as you see her now.

2. YOUR IDEAL MOTHER: Here you can project your image of the perfect Mom.

Your Mother
Your Ideal Mother
EXERCISE 2. YOUR FATHER
1
Who art in Heaven? 15
Whose name is hallowed? 15
Whose kingdom comes? 15
Whose will be done? 15
Whose greatness overwhelms the son? 20
Time for you to tell us! 20

In this exercise you'll be Mind-Scoping and comparing these two personalities:

1. YOUR FATHER: Scope your Dad as you see him now.

2. YOUR IDEAL FATHER: You get a chance to create your very own Dad.

Your Father
Your Ideal Father
Actively reconstruct your life history. First decide on a time in your life (infancy, toddler, first day of school, college, adult, etc.). Now select people who influenced you then.

Your Self Your Ideal Self
Your Mom Your Dad
Your Sister Your Brother
Your Aunt Your Uncle
Your Friend Your Enemy
Your Teacher Your Doctor


choices.text_preview: EXERCISE 2. HOUSEMATE vs. SELF Compare your housemate and you. # Return to previous menu.

prose:
HOME LIFE
Where can't you go again, jiggedy-jog? Where does charity begin? Where do your footsteps turn from wandering on a foreign strand? What is a necessary evil? What is a community of two or more slaves? What is a damnably serious business?

Use the Mirror to find out. The most important Games of Life are often played out at HOME! Here's a chance for you to clarify your thinking about your current housemates. Choose one of the following exercises.

#0EXERCISE 1. HOUSEMATE vs. IDEAL
Compare present and ideal.
#1EXERCISE 2. HOUSEMATE vs. SELF
Compare your housemate and you.
# Return to previous menu.

EXERCISE 1. HOUSEMATE vs. IDEAL
1
YOUR HOUSEMATE: A pessimist once said that "living together is like a field of battle and not a bed of roses."

What's your opinion? Type in the name of someone with whom you live or have lived.

YOUR IDEAL HOUSEMATE: Then there was the optimist who claimed that "living together is made in heaven and is a partnership consummated on earth."

Can you fantasize a bed of roses with 2 BR, 2 Bth, F.Pl, pool? Type in the name of your ideal housemate.

EXERCISE 2. HOUSEMATE vs. YOURSELF
1
HOUSEMATE: Said the cynic, "The people people have for friends, your common sense appalls. But the people people live with are the queerest folk of all." Is this true for you?

Type in the name of someone with whom you live or have lived.

title_hint: YOURSELF: Now let's see how good a housemate your housemate has
prose:
YOURSELF: Now let's see how good a housemate your housemate has.

Type in your own name and we'll Mind-Scope both of you as housemates.
George Herbert said, "The best mirror is an old friend." But now, lucky you, your loyal MIND MIRROR comes to your side. Here you can reflect your thoughts about any of your current housemates.

Housemate Housemate Playing
Housemate Sad Housemate Happy
Housemate Drunk Housemate Stoned
Housemate In AM Housemate In PM
Housemate Ill Housemate Working
Ideal Housemate Worst Housemate


choices.text_preview: EXERCISE 2. PAST LOVES Compare your first and best loves. # Return to previous menu.

prose:
MATING GAME
Finally it's arrived!

An Intelligence Appliance that helps us understand that most mysterious of all events--the Love Game.

Anatole France warned that, "Lovers who love truly do not write down their happiness." But that doesn't apply to you.

Please choose one of the following exercises in the Art of Love.

#0EXERCISE 1. CURRENT LOVES
Compare your love and your ideal.
#1EXERCISE 2. PAST LOVES
Compare your first and best loves.
# Return to previous menu.

EXERCISE 1. CURRENT LOVES
1
As Herbert Hupfeld lyricized, "It's still the same old story, A fight for love and glory, A case of do or die. The world will always welcome lovers, As time goes by."

Let's give the world a look at how you're doing right now.

YOUR LOVE: Type in the name of your current love. If you don't have one, then scope your most recent love.
YOUR IDEAL LOVE: According to the Bible, "Perfect love casteth out fear." So be reckless, rash, courageous! Type in the name of your IDEAL LOVE.

EXERCISE 2. PAST LOVES
1
Do you dare look back at the loves of yesterday? Do you agree with Arthur Symons, who lamented, "I cannot, having been your lover, stoop to being your friend?"

Do you sigh when Byron whispers, "The fruits and flowers of love are gone; the worm, the canker, and the grief are mine alone!" Let's perform a quick scan of your lurid past.

1. YOUR FIRST CRUSH: Think of the first person on whom you had a steamy crush. Don't be afraid of going weak at the knees!

2. YOUR BEST LOVE: Scope the best love of your life and watch what happens!

Your First Crush
Your Best Love
Please don't listen to people like Dorthy Parker who go around saying, "Scratch a lover and find a foe."
Mind-Scope yourself and all or any of your loves--past, present, fantasized.

Your Self A Recent Date
Your Love Tonight's Date
Your First Love Your First Date
Your Ideal Love Your Ideal Date
Your Best Love Your Husband
Your Worst Love Your Wife


choices.text_preview: EXERCISE 2. JOB CHOICE Contrast two dissimilar jobs. # Return to previous menu.

prose:
CAREER PLANNING
The eternal question. "What do you want to be when you grow up?" The choice of a career is often made irrationally, blindly, without accurate reflection.

In this section you can organize your thinking about this important option.

Choose one of the following exercises.

#0EXERCISE 1. CAREER CHOICE
Compare your main career choices.
#1EXERCISE 2. JOB CHOICE
Contrast two dissimilar jobs.
# Return to previous menu.

EXERCISE 1. CAREER CHOICE
1

Are you ready for some locker room advice from Sir William Osler? 40 He said, "One's career is the open sesame to every portal, 40 the great equalizer, 20 the true philosopher's stone which transmutes all the baser metals of human life into... 60 GOLD!!!" 15

PRIMARY CAREER: Type in the name of your primary career choice.

title_hint: SECONDARY CAREER: Type in the name of your secondary career choice
prose: SECONDARY CAREER: Type in the name of your secondary career choice.

EXERCISE 2. JOB CHOICE
1
"Nor crown, nor scepter would I ask, but from my company's will.
By day, by night
To ply the task,
Her cup of bliss to fill."
<,21>- John Quincy Adams

Now unleash your vocational fantasy.

BEST JOB: Enter the job title of the position that, in your heart of hearts, attracts you the most.

title_hint: WORST JOB: Enter the job title of the least attractive job you can think of
prose: WORST JOB: Enter the job title of the least attractive job you can think of.
Scope careers (from this list) you wish to review. Remember, MIND MIRROR, unlike well meaning vocational counselors, gives you no answers, just reflections to stimulate and expand your thinking.

Engineer Executive
Salesperson Nurse
Physician Accountant
Hacker Truck Driver
Psychiatrist Minister
Housewife Husband


choices.text_preview: EXERCISE 2. YOUR TEAMMATES Compare two colleagues. # Return to previous menu.

prose:
WORK ARENA
Much of life's unhappiness is generated on the job--office politics, poor communications, faulty management skills. Just plain human myopia.

But some of the highest moments in life can occur when you are part of a team that is meshing smoothly.

MIND MIRROR can be a valuable tool in clarifying thoughts and improving communication in the Work Arena. Choose one of the following introductory exercises in office politics.

#0EXERCISE 1. THE BOSS!
Compare your boss with another.
#1EXERCISE 2. YOUR TEAMMATES
Compare two colleagues.
# Return to previous menu.

EXERCISE 1. THE BOSS!
1
"The business of the samurai consists in reflecting(!) on his own station in life, in discharging loyal service to his master, in deepening his fidelity in association with friends, and, with due consideration to his own position, in devoting himself to duty above all."
<,16>- Yamaga Soko 1622-1685

See saw, Marjorie Daw, she shall have a new master. She must have a penny day, because she can work no faster.

1. YOUR BOSS: Scope your boss as you see him or her today. If you don't have a boss, do the last boss you had.

2. YOUR IDEAL BOSS: Scope your ideal boss. The sky's the limit!

Your Boss
Your Ideal Boss
EXERCISE 2. YOUR TEAMMATES
1
Who has said it better than William Shakespeare? "Friendship is constant in all other things save in the office and affairs of love. Therefore, let every eye negotiate for itself and trust no agent." - from MUCH ADO ABOUT NOTHING.

Here you Mind-Scope the person with whom you work most closely and compare that person with your ideal teammate.

1. COLLEAGUE: Scope the person you work with most closely.

2. IDEAL COLLEAGUE: Scope your ideal colleague.

Colleague
Ideal Colleague
Here you can review your relationships with teammates, coaches, rivals in the game. Select the persons or situations you wish to Micro-Scope.

Top Boss Boss's Boss
Boss Stressed Supervisor
Boss Relaxed Supervisee
Colleague Receptionist
Underling Product Mgr.
Sales Mgr. Ideal Worker


choices.text_preview: EXERCISE 2. PROSPECTIVE EMPLOYER Compare an employer and yourself. # Return to previous menu.

prose:
DECISION MAKING
We do tend to return to the eternal decision-making dilemma of Hamlet. "To be or not to be: that is the question: Whether 'tis nobler in the mind to suffer the slings and arrows of outrageous fortune, or to take arms against a sea of troubles..."

Effective decision-making can be achieved by precise thinking and clear communication.

Here's the first challenging decision. Select one of these exercises.

#0EXERCISE 1. SCOUTING A CLIENT
Compare your client and yourself.
#1EXERCISE 2. PROSPECTIVE EMPLOYER
Compare an employer and yourself.
# Return to previous menu.

EXERCISE 1. SCOUTING A CLIENT
1
Now you're a salesman. "And for a salesman there is no rock bottom to the life. ... He's a man way out there in the blue, riding on a smile and a shoe shine. And when they start not smiling back--that's an earthquake. ... A salesman has got to dream, boy. It goes with the territory." - Arthur Miller

YOUR CLIENT: Enter the name of a client who will make an important decision about or with you. Scope your impression of him or her.
YOURSELF: Enter your name. Use the same name here as you will when you introduce yourself to the client. Scope the way you wish to present yourself.

EXERCISE 2. PROSPECTIVE EMPLOYER
1
Suppose your big decision involves how to go about getting a great job. Use the Micro-Scope to help create your game plan for this event.

THE EMPLOYER: First type in the name of the prospective employer who will make the crucial decision. Scope how your scouting reports suggest this person will perform.
YOURSELF: Type in your own name and Mind-Scope how you wish to perform during your job interview.

title_hint: It's a bad plan that admits no modification." (Maxims of Publius Syrius, 1st cent. B.C.)
prose:
"It's a bad plan that admits no modification." (Maxims of Publius Syrius, 1st cent. B.C.)
Look over this list. Select players and game situations to Micro-Scope.

Client Rival
Employer Promotion
Your Product Firing
Rival product 1 Hiring
Rival Product 2 New Location
Target Market Ideal Location

choices.text_preview: EXERCISE 2. RIVALS Compare your company's rivals. # Return to previous menu.

prose:
COMPANY STYLE
Environment influences behavior. The organization for which you work can have an enormous influence on your life and your well being.

Institutions, like people, vary in personality. In this section you will learn how to scope your work environment and your responses to it.

Choose one of the following exercises.

#0EXERCISE 1. YOUR COMPANY
Compare your company and your ideal.
#1EXERCISE 2. RIVALS
Compare your company's rivals.
# Return to previous menu.

EXERCISE 1. YOUR COMPANY
1
The sages through the ages have known the importance of "The Company."

Miguel de Cervantes said, "Tell me Thy Company and I'll tell thee who thou art."

A wise Celt suggested that you should always "except" the present Company. Let's find out how you feel about The Company you keep.

YOUR COMPANY: Type in the name of the organization you work for. Mind-Scope its style and personality.

title_hint: Type in the name of the company you'd like to create
prose: Type in the name of the company you'd like to create.

EXERCISE 2. RIVAL COMPANIES
1
"While the law of competition may be sometimes hard for the individual, it is best for the race because it insures the survival of the fittest. We accept and welcome, therefore, great inequalities of environment, the concentration of business, industrial, and commercial power in the hands of a few, and the law of competition as essential for the future progress of the race." (Andrew Carnegie.) Strap on your sword of paranoia and scout the competition.

RIVAL COMPANY 1: Type in the name of an institution which is a rival of yours.

title_hint: RIVAL COMPANY 2: Type in the name of another institution which rivals yours
prose: RIVAL COMPANY 2: Type in the name of another institution which rivals yours.

You can spend many profitable hours screening your thoughts about your work environment. Then you can compare the department with the people who work in them. Does shy Stewart belong in sales?


Sales Dept. Production

Manager Ideal manager

prose:
LIBERAL ARTISTRY
MIND MIRROR can be a useful tool in enjoying and understanding literature, writers and artists.
The key: the student (and even the intelligent teacher) becomes an active participant in the learning adventure. Your understanding of books, characters, plot development, authors, and artists changes (measurably) from week to week.

Choose one of the following exercises.

#0EXERCISE 1. AUTHOR COMPARISONS
Compare two similar authors.
#1EXERCISE 2. CHARACTER COMPARISONS
Compare two dissimilar characters.

1

"Books are the treasured wealth of the world. Authors are a natural and irresistible aristocracy in every society, and more than kings or emperors exert an influence on mankind."
<,19>- Henry David Thoreau

"It is not all books that are as dull as their readers." - Henry David Thoreau

prose:
PHILOSOPHY and SCIENCE
MIND MIRROR encourages you to clarify and plot your thoughts about the Great Issues. Active creation replaces passive learning. The great philosophers exist only in the minds of humans who choose to think about them. You are invited to perform two basic experiments in interfacing your mind with the Great Ones!

Choose one of the following exercises.

#0EXERCISE 1. FAVORITE PHILOSOPHERS

choices.text_preview: EXERCISE 2. LIFE TRAILS Track your life migration. # Return to previous menu.

prose:
SOCIAL GEOGRAPHY
Geography is destiny. Where you are determines who you are. If you live in Texas, you're gonna be influenced by Texans. If you're a dolphin, you won't be happy in the Sahara. Many people live wretched or dull lives unaware of the powerful effect of environment.

The next two exercises may help you understand the effect of habitat in human life. Choose your first psycho-geographical Mind-Scope:

#0EXERCISE 1. OPTIMUM HABITAT
Compare different environments.
#1EXERCISE 2. LIFE TRAILS
Track your life migration.
# Return to previous menu.

EXERCISE 1. OPTIMUM HABITAT
1
Steven Spielberg picked it up from E.T. 20 The question Higher Intelligence wishes our species to answer: 40 HOW CAN YOU INCREASE YOUR INTELLIGENCE? 30 MOVE TO THE HABITAT WHERE SMARTER ORGANISMS RESIDE. 40

OPTIMUM HABITAT: Type in the name of the town, state, or country that most favors your life goals.
LEAST FAVORABLE HABITAT: Type in the name of the town, state, or country that least favors your life goals.

EXERCISE 2. LIFE TRAILS
1
You can learn to track your movements through life the way physicists plot the trails of moving particles. Then Micro-Scope the characteristics of each habitat. By glancing at these Mind Maps you'll see what your environment is doing to you. And vice versa.

BIRTHPLACE: Type in the name of your birthplace.
PRESENT CITY: Type in the name of the city in which you now live unless it is the same as your birthplace. If it is the same, enter the name of the place you'd most like to move to.

Carl Sagan made CONTACT! 15 Messages from E.T. will now appear! Guess the answers. 40 WHAT IS DESTINY? 45 GEOGRAPHY! 15 WHAT LIMITS INTELLIGENCE? 45 HABITAT! 15 Choose geographical personas to Mind-Scope.

prose:
HISTORY
As Karl Marx said: "History belongs to he who writes it." MIND MIRROR frees you from a passive acceptance of "The Expert's" version of everything and allows you to MAKE UP YOUR OWN MIND.

Now it's YOUR responsibility to write history. You can always re-right it!
Use the CURSOR to select a trial run.

#0EXERCISE 1. PRESIDENTIAL CAMPAIGN
Compare two candidates.
#1EXERCISE 2. HISTORICAL FIGURES
Compare two All Star Heroes.
# Return to previous menu.

1
One person knows why America chose Reagan over Mondale in the 1984 election, Carter over Ford in 1976, and Gary Hart over George Bush in 1988. Who?... You do! Think of a presidential campaign (past, future, or imagined) you wish to Mind-Scope.

FIRST CANDIDATE: Type in the name of the first candidate.

Comparing religious beliefs can lead to lively discussions! MIND PLAY can help clarify ideas about this thrilling field of thought and faith.

Aristophanes said it. "Under every stone lurks a politician."

Artemus Ward quipped, "I am not a politician, and my other habits are good, too." This became a favorite quotation of John F. Kennedy.

prose:
POP STARS OF PRINT
George Mason (1725-92) said it well. "The freedom of the press is one of the great bulwarks of liberty, and can never be restrained but by despotic governments."

Anthony Trollope claimed that the "periodical press is the 10th muse, a powerful reflection of your state of mind."

EXERCISE 1. MAGAZINE COMPARISON
1
As Freud said in Psychopathology of Everyday Life, "Ze magazine you read is ze royal road to ze unconscious mind."

Carl Jung, never to be upstaged, retorted, "Ze magazine on the patient's bed table reveals ze deepest secret of ze collective unconscious."

THE MAGAZINE YOU'VE READ THE MOST: Zo! Please type in the name of the magazine you've read the most in the last year.
COMPARISON MAGAZINE: Please type in ze name of another magazine you read often so we can submit both to ze remorseless lens of the Thought Magnifier.

A conservative Frenchman complained, "Homer is new and fresh this morning, and nothing, perhaps, is as tired and old as today's newspaper." Who's Homer?

"A government without newspapers, or newspapers without government? I should not hesitate for a moment to prefer the latter." (Thomas Jefferson.) You're the only one who can settle this debate.

YOUR FAVORITE NEWSPAPER: Please forget Homer and enter the name of the newspaper you read most often.
COMPARISON MAGAZINE: Please enter the name of another newspaper to compare with the one you usually read.

prose:
COMPUTER GAMES
Was it Alan Turing who said, "The Computer Business in 1986 is like the Auto-Mobile Industry of 1921?"

You got big manufacturing companies, brash Detroit executives without a clue, nerd engineers, brigades of salesmen, armies of enthusiast-users who are "into computers," and of course the business users. They all know the applications of the personal Electronic Auto-Mobile. Or do they?

The Computer Industry became The Computer Culture in 1990 when everyone between 16 and 36 had learned to drive a PC. Most just wanted an affordable, efficient tool for reflecting thoughts.

This marked the end of the Computer Tycoons--the legendary hackers, the go-go entrepreneurs, the kid geniuses.

1
By 1990 Personalized Computers clearly were extensions of the mind--indispensable head-tools to clarify thinking and communication. Everyone, save a few lovable wood-pulp eccentrics, drove around in customized headware programs monitoring their private MOS's (Mind Operating Systems).

Recall the quaint applications software of the 1980's! Unwieldy. Impersonal! Lumbering data-trucks.

THE SPORTING LIFE
According to Wellington, "The battle of Waterloo was won on the playing fields of Eton. And it was lost on the playing fields of France."

At a time when heroes are in short supply, athletes and home-town teams serve not only as role models, but also as totems and symbols for tribal rivalries and village myths.

prose:
FLIGHT PLANS
1
NOVICE: Play within large win-orbit on Mind Maps. Step-by-step "Head-Coaching" directs you towards your thought-goal.

EXPERIENCED: Small win-orbits on Mind Maps. Faster play. Optional access to Mind Maps. No "Head-Coaching."

WIZARD: No win-circle. Fly by the seat-of-your-intuition. Mind Maps available only after you've completed a realm.

<,6>^/ LIFE IN THE WOMB /

<,4>^/ CONCEPTION /
1 ________________________________________

CONCEPTION
The Adventure starts at the beginning--your conception of your conception. How did maneuver through the thrill packed Fallopian Tube?

One night not so long ago your Father introduced 500,000 spermatozoa into your Mother's Re-Creational Tract. One of these little fellows is going to make up half of you, .

Meanwhile, up in the hatchery, your Mother's body is selecting a wonderful, once in a life time egg. This egg is going to make up the other half of you.

LIFE IN THE WOMB
Relive the most dramatic nine months of your life. Recapitulate the stages of genetic evolution--from a single cell to a squirming, mobile, human-computer.

Still glowing from conception, you're lazily drifting down the slow moving Fallopian River on your Vesicle Raft. You're looking for a spot on the uterine bank to drop anchor, implant and spend time ashore growing an automobile body to transport you around the planet earth.

You're all alone in your tissue cocoon when suddenly you feel a flaming ecstasy in your mid-section. Ooh! Ooh! Half of your body is pulling away with an orgasmic shudder. O !

BIRTH DECISIONS
Moving day! Time to bid farewell to uterine bliss and face the adventures of Life on Planet Earth. Time to be born!

Hormonal sirens slashing. Neuro-alert signals flashing! ENTRY STAGE COMMENCING.

Whoosh! Watch out! Water bags emptying! Atmospheric pressure building! Contractions buffet Fetal Capsule! ALL LABOR SYSTEMS A-OK! Squeezed by heavy gravity glove! Uterine module slowly tumbles head forward facing EXIT PORT! Pressure building! Brain buzzing with excited messages from Uterine Central! Light at the end of the tunnel! Target planet in sight! COMMENCE FINAL ENTRY COUNTDOWN.

CRIB ADVENTURES
Your first introduction to the sponsors of your Life Show. Meet Mom and Dad!

Slam! Bang! Noise! Bright lights! Oceans of new sensation. Waves of cold, dry air! Warm hands! Jiggle-joggle! Warm, soft Mommy body. Round, satin breasts. Sucking sweet juice of life. Sleep. Wake. Sleep. Wake.

Suddenly you are lifted up, bundled, lugged to a noisy, metal screaming car and brought home. You hear Mommy saying something you don't understand. "Doesn't little look cute lying in the crib?"

You're safely nested in your first home.

EXIT THIS REALM
You may leave this realm at any time. If you haven't played a zone, no score will be given.

Neither one--why bother with this life-and-conception business anyway. <CTRL0E> You may be indifferent to it all, , but the truth is that you started out as a sperm and an egg and
Either one--both are great career opportunities! <CTRL0E> I like your enthusiasm. Just for that, , you'll get to be both.  @

title_hint: 3Here you are, , a sperm making your way through the uterus. What kind of a sperm are you?

choices.text_preview: Full of Catholic zeal. Every sperm is sacred, every sperm is great. If a sperm is wasted, God gets quite irate. Swimming frantically, you almost miss the turnoff to the Fallopian T
Worried, thinking over the ominous spermicidal threats that lie ahead. Contraceptive foams. Rubber condoms. Diaphragms. The futility of masturbation! Virginity! Hesitantly, you hea
Quiet, waiting to see what's going on along the danger-strewn path. Pressed along by the surging crowd, you find yourself swept around the corner into the Fallopian Way. You're in
Joyful. Every sperm is wanted, every sperm is good! Every sperm is needed in your neighborhood. Suddenly there it is, the Fallopian Tube turnoff! You go for it! 

prose:
3Here you are, , a sperm making your way through the uterus. What kind of a sperm are you?

#1Full of Catholic zeal. Every sperm
is sacred, every sperm is great.
If a sperm is wasted, God gets quite irate. Swimming frantically, you almost miss the turnoff to the Fallopian Tube.

#3Worried, thinking over the ominous
spermicidal threats that lie ahead.
Contraceptive foams. Rubber condoms. Diaphragms. The futility of masturbation! Virginity! Hesitantly, you head for the Fallopian Tube.

#5Quiet, waiting to see what's going
on along the danger-strewn path.
Pressed along by the surging crowd, you find yourself swept around the corner into the Fallopian Way. You're in the right mucal tunnel. It could be worse.

#7Joyful. Every sperm is wanted, every
sperm is good!
Every sperm is needed in your neighborhood. Suddenly there it is, the Fallopian Tube turnoff! You go for it!


prose:
Your Mommy appears in her dressing gown. She picks you up in a distracted way. She puts you back down in your crib and says:

#1"What a lively little baby."

You kick your toys to the bottom of the crib and attempt to crawl up the side. Lively! She ain't seen nothing yet. Wait 'til you get some legs under you!

#3"What a serious little sweetheart."

You study her as you suck your pacifier with a steady rhythm. Is it possible that this Loch Ness Momster doesn't understand how bad things really are?

#5"What a peaceful little thing."

You sigh contentedly. You watch her idly. You don't understand why she runs around so frantically when it's heaven here in the uterine bliss of the crib.

#7"What a happy baby."

Why not? To tell the truth, you're a bit puzzled by the frowns and jitters you observe in these strange adults. Why don't they enjoy life the way you do?


After having fed and dressed you, Mommy goes to make the morning coffee. "Have a nice day, Mom." Ho hum. What's going to happen next? It's not the most exciting place in the galaxy being stuck here in this crib. But then again, it's not all that bad, either.

"Our cuddly little bundle of bliss looks like E. T." You ARE an Extra-Terrestrial, trapped on this dumb planet. And these poor humans expect you to grow up like them! You can't wai
"Our bouncy little ball of love looks like Robin Williams." He's right. You're smart, funny, rich. You're a movie star. You're a computer game addict. Everyone loves you, R.W.! 

prose:
Your Daddy appears in his robe. Grinning, he looks down at you. He's in a teasing mood. The big wretch pulls your blankie away to get a reaction from you.

#1You grasp your blankie tightly with
both hands.
He slowly pulls your blankie straight up in the air until he pulls you right out of your crib.

#3You start to cry.

He stops grinning and lets go of your blankie. He hands you your nice rattle. You proved your point. Tears and sobs are the best approach to these people.


Seeing that your not making much of a sport of it, he gives you back your blankie and starts making funny faces at you. Why struggle?

#7You squeal with delight at the new
game.
He plays with you for a long time. Your infectious optimism is a trait that will endear you to some and infuriate others throughout your life-time.


<CTRL0F>
After having his fun, Daddy leaves the room to shave his scratchy face and then rush off to one of his play pens somewhere in the mysterious outside. What keeps these grown ups going? What are they looking for in life? You snuggle down in the warm blankets, pondering these cosmic puzzles.

The door opens again. It's Mommy. She comes in and draws open the curtains. It's so bright! She leans over and takes off your wet diapers. She rubs your cold, wet, smelly bottom with sweet-smelling powder. It feels so warm. Then she puts on your diapers and your warm fuzzy clothes, all the while talking to you in a soft, pleasant voice. She's a sweet thing, this Mommy.

In spite of all the confusion and amateur bungling, this Life Business seems to be working out. You're glad to be here. So far!

title_hint: Pressing the (9) key shows a Mind Plot of your performance within this realm
prose: Pressing the (9) key shows a Mind Plot of your performance within this realm.

RITZ RESTAURANT
An ultra-palatial building with French Empire decor and marble columns. The parking lot is jammed with Rolls Royces guarded by uniformed chauffeurs. Two doormen in formal dress stand by the entrance.

BANKERS CLUB
An imposing granite structure with a canopied entrance and a uniformed guard. A bronze plaque reads:
MEMBERS ONLY.

SUBURBAN^ RANCH HOUSE
A garden party is underway. A sign by the mailbox reads: Dr. Max Papert, Dentist. The host, in a blazer, and his wife are greeting guests.

REVIVAL^ CHURCH
A store-front revival meeting. A neon sign advertises: SINNERS REPENT. You hear sounds of wild hymn-singing. A preacher stands at the entrance.

SOUP KITCHEN
A broken-down slum building. Derelicts are coming and going. A woman in a Salvation Army uniform stands by the door.

DEATH-STAR^ BAR
Through the blackened windows of the run-down building, you hear blaring rhythms and raucous merriment. A bouncer in a black leather jacket stands at the entrance.

YUPPIE^ PARTY
As the stylish guests walk up to the house, their names are checked on a list held by a sophisticated Black man wearing a turtle-neck sweater. Music from THE TALKING HEADS blasts from within.

PLAYTIME^ MANSION
The Place of the Beautiful People--starlets,models, movie directors, rock musicians. The mansion is set back from the street. As cars drive up they are checked by six security guards with lists and walkie-talkies.

<CTRL0E>
That's the way! Keep it up!


<CTRL0E>
Not quite. Remember who you are and what you have!


<CTRL0E>
Beware! You only have one key!
Check your Mind Map for guidance.

They're equally unimpressed. One moves to block the door and tells you in a bored tone of voice, "Sorry, private party."


"The Duke is giving a party in honor of a certain Sir Douglas Adams. AVEZ-VOUS UNE CARTE D'INVITATION?" the second doorman inquires politely. Since you don't have a bloody invitation, you hold up your hands in helplessness. The two doormen look at each other and shrug. One deigns to speak. "I am so sorry. The affair is by invitation only." They turn their backs on you and begin a murmured discussion about babble-fish. Nothing to do. You sadly wander off.

"AVEZ-VOUS UNE CARTE D'INVITATION?" the second doorman inquires. You hold out the embossed party invitation. Ah, the power of a piece of paper for these lackeys! Their manner immediately thaws as they bow politely. One takes your card, smiles and says, "Welcome! His Grace, the Duke awaits. He wishes to present you to the guest of honor--Sir Douglas Adams. This way please!"

The RITZ RESTAURANT has a high ceiling, subdued lighting, lush plants. There's a splashy fountain and marble statues. Ahead of you are cream colored walls, pink table cloths, fresh flowers. It's crowded with French Empire chairs and tables. Asplender in black tie and tails, furs and evening gowns, the crowd resembles a gathering of penguins and polar bears. The music of a string quartet plays behind the hum of polite chatter.

At this point an aide to the Duke approaches and with a gracious smile, says, "It's been such a pleasure having you with us, the Duke would like you to have a token of his esteem." He holds out a silver tray with passkeys on it. "Choose any one you care for."

CHOOSE ONE:
A beaming aide to the Duke walks up and announces, "The Duke extends his congratulations. You have won the Zaphod Beetlebrox Medal! Awarded for poise, elegance and improbability!" How about that! Your prize: a life-time membership in the Royal Hunt Club! And you are the guest of honor at an exclusive banquet at Betelgeuse Five.

Oh oh! Sir Douglas Adams rushes up to you with an insane gleam in his eye. He speaks slowly with controlled rage. "Someone seems to have made a terrible mistake. Panic is in the air! I'm afraid that I must ask you to leave." He leads you to the service entrance. The door slams behind you. Out in the Plaza again.

prose:
The Doorman, known as Minsky, comes over to greet you. He doesn't think you belong, but (although he doesn't like to admit it) he's been mistaken before. "May I help you?" he asks politely.

#1"You are so kind," you murmur as you
wait for him to open the door.
He's intimidated. He's been in charge here for a long time and he hates surprises. You continue to wait for the door to be opened.

#3"Nice place," you say timidly. "Do
you mind if I take a look around?"
He does a double take. He's ready to give you the boot, but he remembers the time he gave the bum's rush to Howard Hughes.

#5"I'm kinda down on my luck at the
moment. Could I talk to the boss?"
You didn't have to tell him that you're down and out, he's paid to know that. But you look peaceful. He bends over in a professorial way.

#7You smile confidently and say, "I'd
like to see the manager."
Hmm. He's puzzled. You're obviously not intimidated by his old-world facade. He decides to proceed respectfully.


He clears his throat. "The Bankers Club is for members only," he says. At this moment a limo pulls up to the curb. The driver opens the door and two very important looking men from the Texas Instrument Company get out. Minsky steps in front of you to greet them and show them inside. He returns. "I'm sorry, but the club is private. Come back tomorrow if you wish to see the manager about a membership." So much for the Bankers Club.

He clears his throat. "The Club is for members only," he says apologetically. He shows his surprise as you hand him your Bankers Club Membership Card. He blinks and makes a quick recovery. "But, of course," he stutters. "How nice to see you again." He opens the door and bows as you walk in.

This place reeks of respectability. It's not designed for giggles. High, high ceilings. Marble columns. To the left, comfortable chairs hold members who are reading papers, chatting quietly and ordering drinks from dignified Black servants. The Bar and Dining Room is filled with men and women standing around chatting. A uniformed butler bows; "Welcome to the Republican Party Reception."

As you reach Dr. Max Papert, he greets you in a friendly Rotarian manner. His wife, Sherry, is at a card table passing out "Support YOUR Lakeview PTA" pins. She hasn't seen you at the meetings.


house for a good cause," you say.
They try to place you, but can't. They do know you're important. It's that casual elegance. Your teeth are nice and shiny, too.

They don't recognize you but they respond warmly. They sense you may be one of their kind.

of middle America. "Hi," you say.
They don't know who you are, but that's no problem. The Doc shakes your hand and glances at your gums. The Mrs. holds out the guest book and a pen.

When you explain that you don't have a child in the Lakeview School, the hosts are upset. It seems that the barbecue is to be preceded by a business meeting during which some very important school business will be voted on. Should books like HUCK FINN, ZORK, and FAHRENHEIT 451 be banned from the school library. But you're invited to the Annual Mardi Gras benefit to be held the next weekend. In costume! Big deal.

Mrs. Papert nervously explains that the party is only for members of the PTA. Afterwards, they are voting on some controversial subjects. Like Sex Segregation... oh no... she means Ed Sexucation. When you produce your membership card in the Lakeview PTA, she blushes with embarrassment. While Max beams, Sherry takes you to the living room. The meeting is just about to begin.

The living room is jammed with solemn looking people on folding chairs. Some are even sitting on the floor. You're offered a seat. Towering above you are two large police officers in uniform. The atmosphere is tense. Dr. Max decries the recent crime wave that has led to a demand for more police protection. What crime? Four bicycles stolen. Garbage cans knocked over by delinquent kids or dogs. Many rumors of kids and even parents smoking pot. Loud rock music disturbing the peace. "Sgt. Kapor," says Dr. Papert, "will explain his plan to stamp out this `holocaust' of crime."

"To enter, you must have an official welfare eligibility card stamped by the Social Security Office," she says. Seeing that you don't have one, she adds, "Go to the City Hall first thing in the morning." She's sympathetic but firm.

Can you believe that! Even here, at the bottom of the ladder, there seems to be Social Status Ritual. What an uptight place this is! It must be Planet Earth!

"To enter, you must have an official welfare eligibility card stamped by the Social Security office," she says.

You hold out your welfare eligibility card. She takes it and steps aside saying, "Make yourself at home." You walk into the hall. Are you ready to deal with the bottom of the social order?

The SOUP KITCHEN is jammed with down-and-outers. The hall reeks of boozy breath, unwashed bodies, cigarette smoke and the pungent odor of bean soup. A social worker is on one side signing up derelicts for part-time work. A minister is preaching to a group of gloomy down-and-outers. Some winos are off in a corner passing around a bottle of muscatel. Some wary Blacks are hanging out across the room waiting for something to happen. What's happening, apparently, is you.

The door to the DEATH STAR opens and the sound of heavy metal jolts your ears; bright lights make you squint. A couple of punkers with Mohawks dance out. They're loaded on something wild, laughing and singing. You notice a sign: GAY NIGHT. Tarheel laughs. "Don't mind the sign. That's just to scare away the wimps. But you gotta have an after-hours card to get in." You show him what you have. He shakes his head. "Nice try," he says as you turn to leave.

You notice a sign: GAY NIGHT! Tarheel laughs. "That's just to keep out the heavy trade," he says. "But you gotta have an after-hours card to get in." The door opens and two punkers dance out. One looks like Boy George. The other looks like Elvis Presley--but she's a girl. Tarheel is still waiting. He's amazed when you hand him your after-hours bar card. "Go for it, sport," he says as he opens the door and waves you in.

As you enter, a blast of heavy metal hits you! Whoom! You look around. The club is an enormous warehouse. Colored light everywhere. To the left is a long curving bar crowded with punkers, mostly male, some feisty females. The dance floor is jammed with couples, some mixed, some male, some unknown. The right wall is lined with people drinking and watching the tense dancing. Way above, the high ceiling is festooned with neon lights which flash and strobe to the sounds. The raw power of the place has your ears and eye-balls popping. You make your way to the bar.

Tim's smile brightens. "We can't wait to see the program. What does it run on?" You don't know what she's talking about. "What program?" you ask. Her smile fades. "Oh dear, you should go to the gate and ask the guard what to do." You walk down the stairs. The Black smiles knowingly as he shows you out the gate.

An attractive Eurasian man in a robe interrupts. "I'm Tim. We'd like to show you our headware. Did you bring the floppy?" "The what?" you ask. "Oh yeah!" you say, handing over the floppy disk. A group of intelligent-looking, sophisticates gather around. Tim holds the disk aloft whispering, "Just think, here are the electrons that changed the world!" Everyone applauds. "What's it called?" someone asks. Tim replies, "MIND MIRROR, the Game of Life."

It's a white wine and brie scene. Low ceilings. Large fireplace. Lots of bookcases. People slung over low cushions and futons. Graduate school informality. A large portrait of David Bowie by Helmut Newton stares down chillingly. The attractive Asian woman approaches. "I'm Tim," she says, "May I be your guide?" A hallway opens into a high-tech room with a sleek stereo, electric piano, large video screen. And a personal computer! The guests look at you and Tim expectantly. It's obvious that they hope that this game, Mind Mirror, will change their lives.

"In recognition of your social skills, select a key to our city." He holds out a stack of cards for you to choose from.

CHOOSE ONE:
Gooch Turing appears. He takes his pipe out of his mouth and hands you a silver PLAYTIME PIN. "Congratulations! You won the works. A life-time MANSION key. It's all yours! The saunas, the screening rooms, the keys to the Bunny Hutch and the Gigalo Gallery. You're Ultra Sophisticated! Want to go for Decadent?"

Your pulse races as a handsome chic couple motions you over. Some aesthetic- erotic liaison? Is that a radio in her bra or is she just well built? Is that a gun in his pocket or is he just glad to see you? They politely inform you that it's time to go. What have you done wrong? (Check the Mind Map to find out.)

<,4>^/ HIGH CHAIR POLITICS /
1 ________________________________________

HIGH CHAIR POLITICS
Momentous Decisions! Will you accept solid foods? How do you handle the perils of crawling and walking?

You're lying warm and cozy in your crib. Suddenly Mommy lifts you up and carries you bump-bump-bump to the kitchen. She slips you in a HIIIIIIIIGH chair. This place is very jangly. Noise! TV blaring. Dishwasher hiss and clang. Dad, at the table, clashes silverware. Mommy comes over and shoves a syrupy mush into your mouth-piece with a metal shovel!

Grosseroo! Kinky-poo! You're used to natural foods. The soft, warm Noobler-Brand Stuff that you can suck, muck and slurp and burp. Well, let's go along with the gag (gulp).

title_hint: PLAYGROUND ENCOUNTERS OF THE WORST KIND
prose:
PLAYGROUND ENCOUNTERS OF THE WORST KIND
Your first trip to the mysterious jungle called Playground. Bullies! Strangers! Reckless defiance of the Law of Gravity.

title_hint: You're two years old, . Time to explore the mysterious universe outside the home
prose:
You're two years old, . Time to explore the mysterious universe outside the home.

Mom dresses you in new toggers. She's excited. She bags you outside and straps you into the front seat of the car. The mission? To go where no infants have gone before. The park.

The engine startles you with ROAR! Cab jabs ahead like a wounded Leo Lion! You thought kiddy cars were dangerous, but this is reckless madness! Whoosh! Duck, Captain Kirk! That car came too close!

THE BIRTHDAY PARTY
You enter the mad social world of a 4-year old. A birthday party, crowded with potential friends and enemies!

At four, you've reached the pinnacle of courageous exploration and external achievement.

And here's Dadster, himself, carrying your toggers. Apparently you're off to a kid's birthday party. You're led into a room filled with kids.

1 ________________________________________

SCHOOL DAYS
Relive your first day at school. Confront the arcane mysteries of reading and writing! School yard bedlam! Your first report card!

COLLEGE ENTRANCE EXAMS
Here you are tested in seven dimensions of intelligence and creativity. How well can you simulate the role of ?

This is your life! Episode 10.
The RDW-battered members of the Senior Class have been herded into the gym and are sitting at tables, pencils in hand, waiting to meet the Supreme Court of Academic Judgment. College Entrance Exams! The exam monitor talks in a quiet, corporate tone of voice about the importance of discipline, excellence, competitive edge. Your old principal, Dr. Jack D. Ripper, drops by to deliver a moving attack on hedonism, a Communist plot to weaken America's moral and military fiber.

A CAREER IN FILM
Fame, fun, fortune await if you qualify for a job at the Video Corp. How well can you play in this glamorous environment?

The VIDEO CORP studios are located behind high, pink-washed walls. First you are checked by security at the Guard Gates. Inside you find acres of sound stages, film labs, administrative offices, miles of roads and sidewalks bordered by palm trees.
You are sent to the office of Freddie Blazer, the Prez. He's a small, cocky man with a foul tongue. He thinks that people will do anything to get into the movie biz. He wants you to take the legendary Scheftel Film Aptitude Scale, to discover which type of work in the film "industry" you are best suited for.

A LITERARY CAREER
You apply for your first job at a prestigious publishing house. How does perform on the tests and interviews?

EXIT THIS REALM
You may leave this realm at any time. If you haven't played a zone, no score will be given.

<CTRL0F>
Approaching the Hauptman Elementary school off Terry Southern Boulevard, one is struck by the scientific purity of the landscaping. Note the stainless steel fence, the crisp cream-stucco building suggesting the knowledgeable and disciplined Hauptman state of mind. Big moment! You have arrived at the Temple of Knowledge! Within these walls you will find the Sacred Books of Human Wisdom. You're about to become a STUDENT! A scholar! The Nobel Prize? Then? A HI-BUCKS job with a computer firm like Giant IBM or mighty Electronic Arts?

<CTRL0F>
This place reeks of Authority, Tradition, Security. You sense that this is an Approved School. This is the place where the Good Kids go. You're awed. Hey, this is the Temple where the Word comes down. It's the Local Branch Office of the Top Management National Security Firm to teach you what's what. It's a Service Academy where you're a cadet-midshipman selected to receive basic training to become a Good Citizen.

<CTRL0F>
You've seen the TV sit-coms and the teen age movies. You wonder when they'll issue pimples and training bras and break-dance jackets and wire teeth braces. You plan to major in rock lyric writing and professional sports. You'll be trained as a TV anchor person. You'll become an agent.

<CTRL0F>
You're being escorted up to a two story building surrounded by high-wire fences. You're unarmed, equipped only with a small lunch box.
> Inventory of box?
A WOODEN STYLUS WITH A CARBON CORE, A WILTING TUNA SANDWICH (AGAIN!) AND A THERMOS CONTAINING A POTION.
> Look.
BEHIND THE FENCE YOU SEE SCORES OF KID-INMATES. SOME STAND SULLENLY, SOME RUN AND SHOUT MERRILY. NEW ARRIVALS HUDDLE CLOSELY TO THEIR ADULT ESCORTS. YOU WONDER, "AM I READY TO PENETRATE AND NEUTRALIZE THIS NEST OF MIND-CONTROL?"

You're at home. Dinner is over. You're lying in bed reading a school book. You fall asleep. You dream. You dream that you are growing up. Fast! Like Alice in Wonderland. Curves, protuberances, bumps, and dangling appendages appearing on your body. Hair under your arms and between your legs! Light bulbs Turning On in your brain. All you think about is boy-girl! Sperm-Egg! Swollen glands bursting with steamy fluids, pleasantly itchy! Hormone pressure driving you wiiiiiiiiiild! In other words, you're ready for High School....

You've finished the Scheftel Scales. Freddie Blazer throws your test papers in the waste basket. "Psychometrics! I use'em for toilet paper. Only one thing counts in the film biz. Bull..." The phone rings. Freddie listens and lets loose with obscenities too lewd for the home computer market. He winks. "Know what, kid? I've just been fired!" "For bad language?" you gasp. He picks up the phone. "Watch this. Get me MGM," he shouts to the phone. "David, baby! It's a deal. I'll run your studio for you. And I'll bring Rambo." He winks again. "See ya around, kid."

%Where do you think the hallowed headquarters of the Encyclopedia California is located?

#2La Jolla, near the prestigious Salk
Institute and the Great Books Corp.
#4Somewhere in California.

#6Pasadena, near Cal Tech, the Rose
Bowl and the Howard Hughes Library.
#8Cambridge, Mass. near MIT, Harvard's
Widener Library and Boston Garden.

The cab from Logan takes you under the tunnel, past the Garden and crosses the Charles River at Mass. Ave. Encyclopedia California! A city block of red brick buildings face the street concealing an inner park: neatly edited lawns, tenured elms, pedantic walks. You report to Jimi Mott, a comely woman, smart, cultured, compassionate (even while trying to kick her nicotine habit). She's a closet computer freak totally dedicated to the notion that screens will replace fiber-based storage systems. She makes her living as Personnel Director of this most snobbish, bookish establishment.

choices.text_preview: Intelligent, thoughtful, well informed. Ideal for "E.C."
You'll be great for library work, file-research and the mail-room.
You're cute but dumb. She has ideas. You obviously don't.
She likes your irreverent, funny style. But you won't last long here.

Silver smoke rings fill the air of Jimi Mott's office. You're both relaxing. Suddenly the telephone rings. Jimi Mott answers, listens, makes a noncommittal response and hangs up. She starts coughing. Then she laughs. "Guess what," she says. "I've been fired!" "For smoking!" you gasp. "No. For disloyalty. They've discovered I've been using the company computer to translate literary classics into electronic books." "Using Mind Mirror on company time!" you say. She nods. "No sweat. It was time for me to go. See you around," she says.
